topics.title: JSP & JSTL
topics.description: JSP (JavaServer Pages) compiles into a Servlet. The container translates JSP to Java source → compiles to bytecode on first access. JSP provides a template-first approach: HTML with embedded Java (scriptlets <% %>) or Expression Language (${expr}). JSTL (JSP Standard Tag Library) replaces scriptlets with XML-like tags: <c:forEach>, <c:if>, <c:choose>, <fmt:formatDate>. JSP implicit objects (request, response, session, application, out, pageContext) are pre-defined variables available without declaration.
topics.story: Imagine building a report using Microsoft Word. You could paste raw data by hand (scriptlet JSP — works but maintenance nightmare), or use Mail Merge fields that auto-fill from a database (EL/JSTL — clean, readable). The Word document is your JSP template. The merge fields are ${variable} expressions. Mail Merge rules (if-then, repeat for each row) are <c:if> and <c:forEach>. The document compiler converts your template into a print-ready document — exactly what the JSP engine does when it compiles JSP into a Servlet.
topics.analogy: Scriptlet (<% Java code %>) = embedding C code inside HTML — powerful but unmaintainable. Expression Language ${user.name} = Thymeleaf/Handlebars placeholder — clean separation. JSTL = Django template tags — <c:forEach items='${list}' var='item'> iterates like {% for item in list %}. JSP implicit objects = PHP superglobals ($session, $request). @include vs <jsp:include> = static copy-paste vs dynamic runtime include. pageContext = the god-object that gives access to all scopes (page, request, session, application).
topics.memoryAnchor: Four scopes (narrowest to widest): page < request < session < application. EL: ${requestScope.user}, ${sessionScope.cart}, ${param.id}. JSTL core prefix c: <c:out value='${x}'>, <c:if test='${cond}'>, <c:forEach items='${list}' var='i'>, <c:set var='x' value='val'>. JSTL fmt: <fmt:formatDate value='${date}' pattern='dd/MM/yyyy'>. Implicit objects: request, response, session, application, out, config, page, pageContext, exception. JSP lifecycle: translation → compilation → loading → instantiation → _jspInit() → _jspService() → _jspDestroy().
topics.firstPrinciples: JSP is syntactic sugar over Servlets. Every JSP compiles to a class extending HttpJspBase (which extends HttpServlet). Scriptlets are pasted directly into _jspService() — they look like code shortcuts but produce unmaintainable output. EL is evaluated at runtime against scoped attributes (page → request → session → application search order). JSTL tags are JSP custom tags that generate Java code in the translated servlet. The MVC separation: Servlet (controller) puts data in request scope → forwards to JSP (view) → JSP reads from EL.
topics.bruteForce: All logic in JSP scriptlets: database calls, business logic, and HTML in one file. Result: a 2000-line JSP that is impossible to test, impossible to read, and breaks every time the schema changes. Mixing concerns is the original Java web sin — it's why PHP got such a bad reputation and why template engines (Thymeleaf, Freemarker) were born.
topics.optimizedApproach: JSP as pure view: zero Java logic, zero scriptlets. Servlet/Controller fetches data, puts it in request attributes, forwards to JSP. JSP reads via ${} EL only. JSTL for iteration and conditionals. Custom tag files for reusable UI components. In modern Spring MVC: Thymeleaf replaces JSP (works without a servlet container, natural templates, testable). JSP is now legacy but still appears in older enterprise codebases.
topics.whenToUse: JSP/JSTL: legacy enterprise apps (pre-2015), maintaining existing JSP-based projects. New projects: Thymeleaf (Spring MVC) or React/Angular (SPA). Understanding JSP is still valuable: servlet lifecycle knowledge transfers to Spring MVC, EL is used in Spring Security annotations (@PreAuthorize expressions), and JSP debugging comes up in maintenance interviews.

examples.title: JSP with JSTL — Product Listing Page
examples.explanation: The Servlet puts data in request scope, then forwards to JSP — this is the MVC pattern. The JSP contains zero Java code: only JSTL tags and EL expressions. <c:forEach varStatus='loop'> provides loop metadata (index, count, first, last, step). <fmt:formatNumber> handles currency formatting without Java code in the view. c:choose/when/otherwise replaces if-else chains. The JSP is inside /WEB-INF/ — protected from direct URL access.
examples.realWorldUse: Legacy banking/insurance systems often have thousands of JSP pages. Reading them requires understanding EL and JSTL. Spring MVC DispatcherServlet forwards to JSP views exactly like this ProductServlet — the RequestDispatcher.forward() mechanism is identical. Thymeleaf th:each='p : ${products}' is the modern equivalent of c:forEach. When migrating JSP to Thymeleaf, the servlet layer stays identical.

examples.title: JSP Lifecycle — What Really Happens Under the Hood
examples.explanation: Every JSP is a servlet — this explains why JSP has the same threading rules as servlets (don't use instance fields for request state). The generated file location in Tomcat's work directory helps debug compilation errors — when a JSP fails, the error message references the generated Java file line numbers, not the JSP source line numbers. Understanding this translation is the key to debugging mysterious 500 errors in legacy apps.
examples.realWorldUse: When you see a stack trace mentioning hello_jsp._jspService() in Tomcat logs, you know it came from a JSP. The Jasper JSP engine in Tomcat recompiles JSPs when changed (jsp:reloadInterval configuration). Spring Boot disables JSP pre-compilation by default — JSPs compile on first access. Troubleshooting: add <%@ page isErrorPage='true' %> to catch unhandled exceptions in JSP, then log ${exception.message}.

problems.title: EL Scope Resolution
problems.description: A servlet sets request.setAttribute('user', 'Alice') and session.setAttribute('user', 'Bob'). A JSP uses ${user}. What value is rendered? Then the servlet sets pageContext attribute 'user' = 'Charlie'. Now what? Explain the scope search order and write code to explicitly target a specific scope.
problems.hint1: EL searches scopes in order: page → request → session → application. First match wins.
problems.hint2: With request='Alice' and session='Bob': request is found first → 'Alice'.
problems.hint3: After pageContext sets 'Charlie': page scope is narrowest → checked first → 'Charlie'.
problems.testCases:
Scope order: page > request > session > application
Scenario 1: Alice (request found before session)
Scenario 2: Charlie (page found first)
Explicit: ${requestScope.user}, ${sessionScope.user}

problems.title: JSTL forEach with Loop Status
problems.description: Write a servlet that creates a List of 5 Student objects (name, grade, passed) and puts it in request scope. Then write the JSTL JSP that displays them in a table with: row number (1-based), alternating row colors for even/odd rows, grade formatted as integer (no decimals), 'PASS' in green or 'FAIL' in red based on the passed field. Show the JSP code and the Java snippet for the expected output.
problems.hint1: varStatus.count gives 1-based row number. varStatus.index gives 0-based. varStatus.last checks last iteration.
problems.hint2: Even row: ${status.index % 2 == 0}. Use c:choose/when/otherwise for pass/fail coloring.
problems.testCases:
1   | Alice   | 92    | PASS
2   | Bob     | 58    | FAIL
3   | Charlie | 75    | PASS
4   | Diana   | 42    | FAIL
5   | Eve     | 89    | PASS
